blocks.text: Your teeth are like a flock of shorn ewes that have come up from the washing, all of which bear twins, and not one among them is bereaved.
2 : , white and evenly paired, with none missing.
Your lips are like a crimson thread, and your mouth is lovely. Your cheeks are like halves of a pomegranate behind your veil.
3 : , , perhaps rosy cheeks glimpsed through a white mesh
Your neck is like the tower of David, built in courses; on it hang a thousand bucklers, all of them shields of warriors.
4 : The image suggests a adorned with a necklace made up of several rows of beads. evokes her regal quality; no such tower is known.
Your two breasts are like two fawns, twins of a gazelle, that feed among the lilies.
4.1–5 : He describes parts of her body, using similes and metaphors.
5 : Elsewhere the man is described as , an erotically suggestive image in which the lilies signify the woman (2.16; 6.2–3).
Until the day breathes and the shadows flee, I will hasten to the mountain of myrrh and the hill of frankincense.
6 : He responds to her exhortation of 2.17. , , the woman's breasts, the woman herself, and the place where the lovers enjoy love's pleasures.
You are altogether beautiful, my love; there is no flaw in you.
Come with me from Lebanon, my bride; come with me from Lebanon. Depart from the peak of Amana, from the peak of Senir and Hermon, from the dens of lions, from the mountains of leopards.

8 : , here and v. 11, and “my sister, my bride” (4.9,10,12; 5.1) are terms of endearment. , , and , peaks in the Anti-Lebanon mountain range, north of Israel, where and make their dens, create a contrast to the accessible mountains of v. 6 and symbolize the woman's inaccessibility and the awe she inspires in him.
You have ravished my heart, my sister, my bride, you have ravished my heart with a glance of your eyes, with one jewel of your necklace.
9 : , either “stirred my heart” or “captured my heart.” He speaks of love in terms of conquest, describing his feeling of being overwhelmed by her as something she has done to him.
How sweet is your love, my sister, my bride! how much better is your love than wine, and the fragrance of your oils than any spice!
Your lips distill nectar, my bride; honey and milk are under your tongue; the scent of your garments is like the scent of Lebanon.
11 : , i.e., the fragrant cedars.
A garden locked is my sister, my bride, a garden locked, a fountain sealed.
4.12 : , his private garden for his exclusive pleasure.
Your channel is an orchard of pomegranates with all choicest fruits, henna with nard,
nard and saffron, calamus and cinnamon, with all trees of frankincense, myrrh and aloes, with all chief spices—
a garden fountain, a well of living water, and flowing streams from Lebanon.
13–15 : She is a paradisiacal garden, where exotic spice-bearing plants and trees from such faraway places as Arabia, Africa, and India grow side by side.
Awake, O north wind, and come, O south wind! Blow upon my garden that its fragrance may be wafted abroad. Let my beloved come to his garden, and eat its choicest fruits.
4.1–5.1: The man's first long speech. The man's mode of speaking about love is to look at his lover, tell her what he sees and how it affects him.
4.12–5.1 : A cluster of metaphors creates a picture of his lover as a fragrant garden, full of edible delights.
16 : The woman interrupts him, first inviting the winds into her garden to whet the man's appetite by stirring up its sweet smells, then inviting her lover to an erotic banquet.
